The most important time in your whole life to fast from your phone is when you wake up in the morning. And they have to immediately take action. So we get people to go and pick up their toothbrush as soon as they wake up and start brushing [music] their teeth, splash cold water on their face, and come back and make their bed. Your brain wakes up, it's seeking for dopamine, it's seeking to attack the day. If it goes straight into the phone, you're really setting yourself on a much more difficult journey. So that's your morning phone fasting, at [music] least 15 minutes first thing in the day. Take action. In the evening, we then need a minimum of a 60-minute [music] period where again we're physically away from the phone. So during that period of time you might be cooking and eating, you might be exercising, socializing. Then you're developing like a very clear structure [music] into your life where you're getting away from the quick dopamine of the phone. Fundamentally, the phone is the main thing in our lives hijacking the system. If you get away from the phone more, you're just going to have way more motivation.

## Story 00201
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @AliAbdaal
- **Source:** yt_transcript_masterclass_ali_abdaal
- **Video ID:** 

All right, so the new year is coming up and you might be tempted to set annual goals. So my hot take on annual goal setting is that I don't like setting annual goals. See the thing with annual goals is that it's very easy to be like this is my goal for 2026 and you set that goal in January and then by like March or April the goal kind of becomes irrelevant because you'll have hopefully taken some kind of actions towards it and then the goal will change as a result of the actions you take. Either that or you'll just get to April and you'll have forgotten that you actually set the goal in the first place cuz like a year is just sort of too long. I instead prefer to set quarterly goals, which I like to think of as quarterly quests or QQ's. And I think of them as quarterly quests because [music] quest, you know, thinking of a goal as a quest is just just frames it in a bit more of a fun way. And that's kind of the whole thesis of feelgood productivity that if you can kind of find a way to make productivity more fun, more enjoyable, you're actually more likely to do it. And therefore, you're going to be more productive and more creative and all that fun stuff. So, I instead work in sort of 90-day quarterly quests. You know, for me, I split life up into work and life. And so I have a main quarterly quest for work and a main quarterly quest for life. And so for that you kind of want to ask yourself in the next 90 days in the work domain of my life, what is the one thing if I just did one thing and only one thing that would have the most impact? In my personal life, what is the one thing and only one thing that if I just did that and everything else stayed the same? What's the one thing that would have the most impact? Cuz a major mistake people make when it comes to setting goals is actually trying to set way too many of

## Story 00202
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @AliAbdaal
- **Source:** yt_transcript_masterclass_ali_abdaal
- **Video ID:** 

All right, we have a question here from Sparta Studio, of all people. How can you come up with hit video ideas that can help you make money while you sleep? So, um I'm assuming this person is referring to uh YouTube AdSense because if you make videos on YouTube, then you make money while you sleep through the ads that play before the videos. You can make some money from Instagram, you can make some money from TikTok, you can make some money from YouTube Shorts, but that usually is like tiny, tiny, tiny in comparison to the money you can make for like a long-form with proper YouTube video once you get monetized with the appropriate number of subscribers and watch hours and all all of that jazz. So, then the question becomes, how do you make good videos? Um assuming you've hit the monetization threshold. And the answer is, I don't know. It's like a constant experimentation over time. A lot of it, I think, comes from actually speaking to your audience. So, a big part, like I host various events here in Hong Kong where I live, and I also host various online workshops for my courses and stuff. >> [music] >> And a big part of why I do live events and live online workshops, like I don't have to do it. I don't make money from the live events. We In fact, we lose money from the live events. But a big part of why I do it is because >> [music] >> it gets me close to my target audience. Like the sorts of people who would rock up to an event that I'm hosting to do a talk in Hong Kong and then come up to me afterwards to ask me questions. Usually, those questions are about like, how do I build my business? How do I quit my job? How do I get more financial freedom? How do I get rich? How do I manage my time? I now have the curse of knowledge in that I'm already pretty good at being productive and I'm already pretty good at making money. And so, I often don't appreciate what a beginner to making money needs to know about the topic. And so, when someone asks me the question and I have a conversation with them, that is where I get a lot of my best ideas from to be like, "Oh, wow. Okay, that was a complete misconception that that person had about making money." And I know what the answer is, so I can just make a video about that. And often those videos are the ones that do the best, rather than me trying to come up with a video idea, like for a hit video to be like, you know, what's going to get views. Actually just trying to connect it to what is the question that someone has. Like what's the thing they are struggling with? And how can I help them as much as possible? That tends to result in more hit videos that make money while I sleep, rather than all of the other methods of coming up with video ideas. There are also various other methods of coming up with video ideas. You can look at what's doing well on the platforms, you can look at your competitors, you can see what their top performing videos are. You know, you can do your own version of their videos and stuff, which is why, like, anytime like someone gets a hit video on YouTube, everyone else in the same space basically starts to copy that title and thumbnail and do their own take on that because they realize that that's a good way of getting views and and stuff. So, you can play that game, but personally, I prefer to play the game where I have conversations with people, they ask me questions, and then I make videos about their responses, which is actually a big part of why I'm doing this little series on Instagram cuz it's way easier for me to make videos answering someone's question than it is for me to make videos just coming up with life advice, you know, off the top of my own head. So, hopefully that helps.

## Story 00203
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @AliAbdaal

- **Video ID:** 

If you are currently feeling a little behind in life, this is something that potentially might help. So the other day I was hosting a workshop for my online business school students where we help people start their first online businesses and a bunch of them were saying that they are feeling behind cuz we've got some students that are like making loads of progress and making all this money. And we have some students who are making a lot less progress and you know life got in the way and then you know the dog died and then like something came up and then it was the holidays and you know all these things that make them feel behind. And so what I was saying to our students was that basically everyone's running their own race, right? Like you're going to be in this game for the next 10 plus years if you care about building a successful business, right? So it doesn't really matter if you're I don't know 3 4 5 weeks behind someone else cuz also people are starting out at different starting points. Like for example, if you already have a personal brand and you have a following online, then of course it will be way easier for you to make money quickly compared to someone who is starting completely from scratch. If you are starting a business within a niche where you actually have expertise, skills, and credibility, it'll be way easier for you to make money and you'll do it quicker compared to someone who is starting in a completely new niche, completely from scratch. So, the whole comparison thing, if it's causing you to feel behind, it's just not particularly helpful. Comparison, if it's a form of motivation, like, oh my god, look at that person over there. They've already made $10,000 within the first month. That's really inspiring and we learn from them. That's totally fine because we're using the comparison as a way of motivating ourselves and as a way of learning. But often unfortunately we find with our students and maybe for you as well that when you compare yourself to someone who's ahead of you rather than it feeling inspirational it starts to feel demotivating because what you take away from that is that you suck and that you're going to suck forever and then it's not really a helpful place to be. So our students found that helpful on the workshop. Maybe you find it helpful as

The earth is blasted by the nuclear heat of the sun with the equator getting hit hot hardest, making hot air rise there as it cools out along the edge of space before coming back down, creating a big scale convection current that repeats in a pattern all up and down. And because the earth is a sphere that spins, those cylindrical convection currents of air on the surface twist clockwise north of the equator and counter below. This is the Coriolis effect, creating coiled currents of air everywhere, which is cool, but also physics trying to sneak back into the video. And this isn't a physics video. All that matters is this creates the pretty stable pattern of trade winds and if you're planning a port, you need only know which way does the wind mostly blow. [35.4] you expected. Sometimes the delay isn't

Nobody cares about your story. This building behind me is where Samir and I spent the majority of our 20s. Samir's dad is in the fashion business and this is one of his warehouses and he moved a bunch of boxes aside and agreed to let us use it as our first office. We're building the lacrosse network and Samir comes up to me and he suggests that we tell a story about a major trade that happened in pro lacrosse. And I looked at him and I said, "People aren't really talking about this. Sure, there's some tweets about it and there's some articles written, but no one's talking about this trade, so we shouldn't be talking about it. No one cares. And he said to me, "It's our job as storytellers to make people care." And that flipped a switch in me, and I hit the road, and I spent a month living with Paul Rabel, documenting his life. And it went from a story about a trade to a deeply personal story about a guy who would go to incredible lengths to win. And that series ended up becoming one of our most successful on the channel and truly set the tone for who we would become as storytellers. And so the gap between people caring and not caring is the story that you tell. So if you're thinking about telling a story about your own life and you're discouraged thinking no one cares, then you're actually on to something. That's where you want to be because that's where all the opportunity

Did you know that there's 95 million photos and videos uploaded to Instagram every day? There's 23 million Tik Toks uploaded every day and 20 million YouTube videos uploaded every single day. There has never been more content than right now. So, if you're a creator and you're thinking about how to stand out, I don't want you to think necessarily about views. I want you to think about how people are spending their minutes online. We break this up into three different types of minutes. The first is forgettable minutes. I assume there's a lot of you here who spend a lot of time scrolling Instagram reels. It's possible you do that when you're like in line for the bathroom or on the subway or on an airplane and two days later you can remember that you were on Instagram at that time, but you probably can't remember what you watched. Then there's regrettable minutes. Regrettable minutes is like when you spend a ton of time scrolling and you want that time back. It feels kind of gross and icky. Then there are memorable minutes. With the abundance of content online, we need to start striving to create memorable minutes, content that people will remember, stories that people will remember. And that takes a level of honesty and it takes a level of actually having something to say and something to share with the world. If you're only thinking about what would be strategically a good video, there are so many videos uploaded every minute to these platforms. That video is probably already being made. Forget about that one. Dig deep and think about what you honestly have to say. Say that.

He won an Oscar for traffic. >> So he decided to take me out to lunch here at Terrace Mexican Grill. And that lunch changed my life. and we would come here every day for lunch. But on this day, he wanted to ask me why I was so frustrated. I started by explaining to him that I felt like everything we were doing here in this editing room was moving really slow. It didn't feel like the creative energy back in film school where I would come up with an idea, grab a camera, I would write it, direct it, produce it, edit it, act in it. I could do everything, upload it to the internet, and send it to my friends in like a week. So, he gave me the best piece of advice I've ever gotten. He said, "Right now, you're an assistant editor, and you're pretty good as an assistant editor, and I'd hire you again as an assistant editor. Then I'd recommend you to my friends as an assistant editor, and you'll keep getting jobs as an assistant editor until maybe one day, if you're lucky, you could be me. Do you want that?" And I said, "No, I don't want that at all. I want to I want to do something with a lot more creative energy." He said, "So, whatever is in your head, whatever that thing is you just described to me, go do that. Figure it out. Is it safe to leave close to a power distribution center or high voltage power lines? Yes. Yes, it is. I mean, it is safe as long as proper clearances per code and standards are met. I'm talking to those people who think radiation from power plants messes with their chakras, causes cancer or something. See, we have lower voltage power lines all around us at home radiating at us like this piece of wire that with a load radiates electromagnetic fields and without it just electric fields because there's no current. But either way, with or without there is always a safe distance to the power lines. The no no distance starts from directly touching the wires all the way to a point where the induced energy or discharges from the lines are not detectable by humans. I'm not moving now touching my LED circuit and there is some glow from induced AC voltages. You simply walking around generates more electrical energy on your body than any external radiations from power lines. I'll be walking on my treadmill. You see how much brighter the LED glows with my every step? Now, I'm not trying to say moving around gives you cancer. I'm saying if just being active moves tons of charges through your body, that doesn't give you cancer. You shouldn't worry about the tiny induced voltages from being close to high voltage lines either.

And then you bring another magnet close, which is fine. The south likes to be close to the north and north likes to be close to the south side. So, this is a stable position and it doesn't want to move anymore. But then you get more aggressive and push the magnet further into the magnet hole and put energy into it. So, at some point, you see the south pole is attracted by the north poles in front of it and the north pole is repelled by the other north poles behind it. So, it accelerates forward and shoots out from the other side. So basically you put energy into it to put the magnet between the other magnets and then it shoots out releasing the energy you already put into it. a big part of writing is giving every character a unique voice and a lot of the time people focus on characters having different accents or using specific words or speaking patterns right but actually I find the most important way to do this is to give them a different role in the scene or a focus in their dialogue like in Game of Thrones Tyrion often brings up the cruelties of others how things affect outcasts like himself while Tywin might focus on the damage something does to the family name same upbringing they use the same sort of words but they sound very different one character might be more Reckless while the other pushes for caution it's all about giving your characters different emphasis

- **Video ID:** 0VoNODaw4uw

writing dialogue that sounds natural is really hard but it can help to think about how when we're talking we often don't respond directly to what someone says like imagine if two people are talking about what to have for dinner and one person says what do you feel like Thai Japanese pizza and the other person will say I think the pizza down the road is the cheapest they're both talking about the same thing but from slightly different perspectives the first person is interested in what type of food they're eating while the other person is concerned about saving money and it's because of that they don't respond directly they don't say yes I would love to go out for dinner I would like Japanese they talk about the more important issue in their mind on that topic we also tend to make leaps in a conversation instead of saying yes I would like Japanese we say there's this Japanese place in town skipping the direct response or leaving it to subtext so think about the unique perspective a character might bring to the conversation how I might change their responses or where they guide the conversation and if you'd like more tips like this there's a whole two chapters on dialogue and subjects and on-running World building volume 3 the series is sold over 100,000 copies and it's available right now down in the comments

- **Video ID:** 1D1OMcXkKG0

okay so you want to write horror you want to write something scary great well as Stephen King once said horror is in everything before you open the door in other words it's everything before you actually see the Monster before you see the scary thing how many times have you seen a horror movie where you actually finally see the thing and you go uh yeah I mean I guess it's scary but it was more scary when I hadn't seen it so instead of making it scary by thinking about its eyes or its teeth or it's weapons instead think about the clues left behind your characters actually discover what's the atmos feere the feelings that those Inspire because often times the horror that your readers are going to have at their imagination is better than anything else you can put on the page stretching out that anticipation allows the reader to run wild with their imagination and this applies to tense scenes where someone's following them you give the clues and the hints rather than showing them you show them that there is Darkness or something's in it not what's in the darkness and if you want more writing tips like this follow me over on YouTube at Hell future me or pick up my on rting World building series which have already sold around 100,000 copies

so I'm writing my own Dungeons and Dragons campaign right now and I want to share a super simple formula for some amazing DND puzzles because let's face it a lot of them are just pretty terrible one player takes over and just other players are excluded well the trick is you decentralize information so let's say the party needs a very uniquely shaped key to get through the door for whatever reason they're trapped in different rooms or they look at a magical wall and see different things the players all have different information that they have to coordinate together so half the party can see what the key needs to look like and the other half of the party has a machine that allows them to print the key that they might need the two sides have to communicate what that key looks like accurately enough that they can print the right key and put it in the door and go through it it's kind of like a stick I've drawn the door symbol what do you mean it's not this and if you want to make it even harder then split up the information even more maybe one player knows the order in which locks need to be unlocked first

don't fall into the Trap of environmental determinism this is the idea that a civilization success culture religion politics are mostly defined by their environment when that's just not the case culture politics language and Society is equally defined by the threats we Face the wars we fight religious movements class systems nearby cultural influences things brought over with trade technology and very importantly migration and colonialism all of which can completely over IDE whichever environmental pressures or barriers there might be after all has Humanity not spent thousands of years trying to eliminate the environmental pressures that would normally shape us we're not just the top of the food chain we're barely even part of it and yet our culture keeps evolving and this is especially the case in a world of increasing globalization if you want more writing tips like this then follow me here or over on YouTube at hell of future me or pick up my on ring and World building series which is already sold over 100,000 copies

the first common way that writers handle power escalation is by integrating it with character arcs that gaining abilities or mastering certain Technologies require specific character traits such as in Brandon Sison Stormlight Archives where the characters of Delona and Kellan slowly attain new abilities like those of the mystical order called the knights radiant but they can only do so in accepting and living up to certain moral ideals Kellan gains new abilities when he accepts that protecting others means he should even protect those that hates this technique usually means that power escalation aligns with the growth of your characters and this can be a really great way to not only ensure that character development is always front and center in your story but that abilities feel earned

killing your main character can be a really powerful story beat but it's also really hard to pull off especially because the reader might feel like well what is there left for me to care about so here are a couple of tips one use the death of your main character as the inciting incident for the stories of other characters in your story like when nid Stark dies in Game of Thrones it Sparks the stories of other characters as they scatter across the world some trying to survive some trying to get Justice nid who we're quite attached to has a real presence in the books from then on and two consider Shifting the narra of momentum an initiative to other characters getting them to do important stuff as well before you do this to make it feel like the story can continue on without them especially if there's unresolved plot reads if you want more writing tips like this then follow me here or over on YouTube at hello future me or pick up my onw writing and World building series which has already sold over 100,000 copies

music has been imagined as magical in some way for about as long as we've had music the idea that hitting the right notes the right words in the right order with the right intonation the right Harmony or Rhythm that it allows us to tap into something more powerful more fundamental about the universe to even connect with the Divine and because of this music Can Be Imagined as a rare kind of magic system with its own rules powers and logic in these kinds of magic systems everything might have its own Melody frequency resonance or note perhaps the ocean goes loud and quiet loud and quiet like the tides coming in and out or a bonfire might be Up Tempo and high pitched with unexpected notes like at crackling while a candle flame has the soft consistent wavering tune before suddenly going out and if you want to learn more about this kind of magic system go check out my video on musical magic systems

difficult to make the reader want Exposition because it's just thrown at the reader to grease the path of the main character into their Adventure explaining the magic system or the world and their place in it as the hero it's just given to them it's not necessarily inherently interesting one way to circumvent this is to use Exposition as payoff in the narrative by placing obstacles between it and the main character or framing it as a mystery after all the answer to a mystery is essentially Exposition a fantastic example of this is the Matrix the question what is the Matrix is set up from the beginning and Neo spends the first 30 minutes investigating and unraveling this mystery these obstacles and this element of mystery means that when Neo finally meets morus the exposition surrounding the Matrix feels more like a payoff to everything that has come before if you want more writing tips like this then follow me here or over on YouTube at Hell future me or pick up my onw writing and World building series which is already sold over 100,000 copies

hard magic systems often boil down to three things their limitations weaknesses and costs these essentially create the rules your magical characters have to obey what limits are there on their powers the most common form of limitation is a vaguely defined limit of strength or training or mental Acumen of the practitioner secondly weaknesses weaknesses in Magic systems can create interesting Dynamics in a story where magic would usually make a character a lot more powerful than those around them L and thirdly perhaps the most common way that people create rules for their magic system is through magic costing something perhaps the most common magical cost is that of bodily energy in The Wheel of Time and The Inheritance cycle doing something with magic exhausts you which is fine until you try and be too magically heroic and you overdose on magical heroin and die if you want more writing tips like this then follow me here or over on YouTube at Hell future me or pick up my on writing and W building series which has already sold over 100,000 copies

fight scenes are really hard to do well but one way to up the tension isn't just to make characters hit harder more risk of death but to introduce multiple objectives it's not just fighting for their life but they have to get them a guffin or save their friend too in iane there's this great scene where VI and Jinx are fighting but it's made more tense by Vi wanting to save her sister bring her back from the brink but also every character in the scene needs these magical balls which are being tossed around and fought over it allows your characters to win on one level and lose on another making the fight seem more interesting and setting up future scenes where you can explore those conflicts more if you want more writing tips like this then follow me here or over on YouTube at Hell future me or pick up my on ring and World building series which is already sold over 100,000 copies

being mindful about word choice and specificity you're going to want to look out for the words very or extremely when describing emotion Stephen King hates adverbs like these because they often mean that you're not quite being specific enough with the emotion you're playing with you're not capturing the scale you intended and we don't tend to get anything more out of being told that someone was very afraid versus afraid and by the way I counted and King only uses these terms 10 times outside dialogue when he's describing things in his book Miser so do go through your book control if and maybe instead of very tired Tri exhausted sleepy drained sick of it these all mean more specific things if you want more writing tips like this then follow me here or over on YouTube at hello future me or pick up my on writing and World building series which is already sold over 100,000 copies

writing tip for Great Character descriptions Vivid character descriptions aren't just a laundry list of their hair color eye killer shoulder shape who knows not only is a laundry list of bland physical features kind of forgettable but trying to force the reader to imagine your character exactly the way that you have is unlike in TV and film kind of a FSE erand so instead consider giving your characters a memorable or distinguishing physical feature that the reader can imagine them around like in William Gibson's burning Chrome here Chrome her childlike face smooth as steel with eyes that would have been at home at the bottom of some deep Atlantic trench that's a really distinct feature about her her eyes and it actually hints at her darker nature as well so what memorable or distinguishing feature does your character have that sets them apart and if you want more writing tips like this follow me over on YouTube at Hell future me or pick up my on writing World building series which have already sold around 100,000 copies

Just let me know. Right about here in Dallas, Texas, American President John F. Kennedy was assassinated on November 22, 1963. The accused assassin was a man named Lee Harvey Oswald. After being held in custody for less than two days, Oswald himself would meet the violent end. According to a subsequent investigation, Oswald had fired three rounds from the Texas school looked upon story. Police recovered three shell casings and a bolt-action rifle from the sixth floor of the building. But the shooting of Oswald sparked immediate suspicions of conspiracy. Oswald's death persists to this day and have driven most Americans to reject the government's conclusion that Oswald acted alone. The arguments for and against conspiracy take many shapes and forms. Far too many in fact to do the maljustice in a single video. Instead, in this video, I want to zoom in and focus on a very narrow slice of this case. Specifically, the events surrounding the Texas Schoolbook Depart Story. What exactly happened inside this building from where the shots that killed the president are believed to have been fired? In September of 1963, a young man named Wesley Frazier received a call from an employment agency. There was a potential job opening at the Texas Schoolbook Depart Story. On September 13, Frazier made his way from Irving Texas, down to Central Dallas. He met with Roy Troulee, the superintendent of the building and was hired on the spot. Back in Irving, Frazier lived with his older sister, the new Randall. A few houses to the street lived a woman named Ruth Payne. On October 14, both Randall and Payne had a cup of coffee at the house of a neighbor. Payne brought along a friend and Russian immigrant named Marina Oswald, the wife of Leoswald. Marina could barely speak in English and had been staying with Payne for a few weeks, partly due to her husband's unemployment. The subject of Lee looking for work and that he hadn't found work for a week came up while we were having coffee. And Mrs. Randall mentioned that her younger brother, Wesley Frazier, thought they needed another person at the Texas Schoolbook Depository where Wesley worked. Payne and Marina returned home, spoke with Roy Troulee over the phone and secured a job at the view on behalf of Leoswald. The following morning, he went down to the book depository and began his first day of work on October 16. So in acquaintance with whom Oswald's wife was currently staying, had a casual conversation with a neighbor. That neighbor happened to have a brother who recently got employed at the book depository. This prompt that his wife to ask the acquaintance to call the superintendent of the building to inquire about the new vacancies. I told Mrs. Payne that to send Oswald down and I would talk to him that I didn't have anything in mind for him of a permanent nature, but if he was suited, we could possibly use him for a brief time. The position was not only temporary, but only existed due to a temporary shortage of staff. It was the end of our fall rush. If it hadn't existed a week or two weeks longer, or if we had not been using some of our regular boys putting down this plywood floor, we would not have had any need for the Lee Oswald at that time, which is a tragic thing for me to think about. But it gets even more tragic because Oswald was not the only one to apply for a job. I might have sent Oswald to work in a warehouse two blocks away. Oswald and another fellow reported for work on the same day, and I needed one of them for the depository building. I picked Oswald. If Oswald was somehow entangled in a conspiracy, and the aim of that conspiracy was for him to become employed at the Booktapal story, this roundabout sequence of events is a very strange way to go about it. As the name implies, the Texas School Booktapal story was in the business of selling books. For about 25 an hour, Oswald's job was to prepare the books for shipping. He did so by filling out forms and transporting cartons of books between the upper floors and the first. The building had three elevators, two staircases, and was seven stories tall. In the northwest corner, one stairway and two freight elevators provided access to all seven floors. A small passenger elevator near the front entrance stopped at level four, while stories one and two were connected by a second flight of stairs. Lacking a driver's license, Oswald relied on Wesley Frazier to carpool between Irving and Dallas every Friday evening and Monday morning. He spent work days at the Rooming House in Dallas while spending the weekends with his wife and daughters in Irving. But on Thursday morning, November 21st, the day before the assassination, Oswald decided to deviate from this routine. I was standing there on the first floor, getting orders in, and Lee said, could I ride home with you this afternoon? ## Story 02950
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @LEMMiNO
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_worldbuild_story
- **Video ID:** 6oY8HIWBS-Y

Just let me know. In the early hours of February the 25th, 1942, major cities along the Pacific coast of the United States were blacked out due to concerns of an incoming enemy attack. As powerful searchlights scoured the heavens, the roaring streets of Los Angeles fell silent. Suddenly, the silence was broken by the deafening sound of explosions. The army had opened fire and a city was under attack. The blackout persisted until dawn and the troubled masses now demanded an explanation, but no one seemed to know exactly what had transpired. Nothing but shrapnel had fallen from the sky and witness accounts were hopelessly at variance. As the war raged on across the Pacific, this frantic episode soon faded from public consciousness. Years later, the story resurfaced under the banner of conspiracy. The true nature of the affair had supposedly been covered up by the government and the battle of Los Angeles has since become a staple of UFO mythology. On the morning of December the 7th, 1941, the Imperial Japanese Navy launched a surprise attack on the American naval base at Pearl Harbor. The devastating attacks and shockwaves throughout the country and in response the United States formally declared war on Japan the following day. A state of war has existed between the United States and the Japanese Empire. As the war progressed, Japanese submarines patrolled the Pacific coast and occasionally sunk a ship within sight of a major coastal city. Alerts and blackouts became routine as fear of a Japanese invasion quickly enveloped the entire western seaboard. Just in three months after the attack on Pearl Harbor, Japan attacked the U.S. mainland. As the sun was setting on February 23, 1942, a Japanese submarine surfaced a few kilometers north-west of Santa Barbara after taking aim at Elwood Oilfield, Gunners' Mandel-Arch Tech Cannon and Open Fire. The shelling lasted for 20 minutes and more than a dozen exploding shells came raining down on the docks and nearby installations. Although the damage was minimal and without casualties, the attack had a significant impact on public fears and the threat of a Japanese invasion now seemed imminent. Due to concerns that the bombardment of Elwood had been a diversion intended to draw attention away from more significant targets, the military maintained a state of readiness that lasted well into the following day. On the evening of February 24, Naval Intelligence issued a warning that an attack could be expected within the next 10 hours, as such the Southern California coast was put on yellow alert. After three hours, the situation somewhat relaxed when the alert reverted back to white, but tensions remained high as repeat attacks could come at any time. At 1.44 a.m. on February 25, three separate radar stations picked up an unidentified flying object approaching the city of Los Angeles. By 2 a.m., the target was roughly 200 kilometers of shore and a few minutes later anti-aircraft batteries were put on green alert and Gunners prepared for the city to be raided. Some 25 minutes later, the UFO had been tracked within 5 kilometers of the city and so the alarm was sounded and Los Angeles was blacked out. Even though the UFO soon vanished from the radar scopes and never reappeared, visual sightings of enemy airplanes now flooded the information center. At 307, the artillery units were given the order to open fire and from this point onwards, the skies above Los Angeles erupted like a volcano. The firing became sporadic as a great variety of targets were sighted all over the city. They ranged from a lone enemy fighter to an entire fleet of up to 200 high altitude bombers. Meanwhile equally competent witnesses failed to see anything at all. They're verging estimations of altitude and speed only confused the situation further. Some thought the planes resembled birds while others insisted they had seen balloons or even a blimp. Although the blackout persisted until dawn, the bear arch itself concluded shortly after 4am by which time more than 1400 rounds of anti-aircraft artillery had been expended. The evening newspapers struggled to make sense of the situation. Initial reports indicated that multiple enemy planes had been shot down across southwest and Los Angeles. A police officer had allegedly seen two planes fall from the cone of the searchlights. However investigations by the local police found nothing but shrapnel and the Western Defence Command announced that no bombs had been dropped and no planes had been shot down. The confusion was only compounded by the lack of agreement between military officials. The Secretary of Navy Franklin Knox, speaking at the press conference in Washington, claimed the incident had been a false alarm and that no planes had been present, further adding that the bear arch had been provoked by nothing more than nervousness and over excitement. Meanwhile the Secretary of War Henry Stimson, assisted up to 15 unidentified planes, had in fact flown over the

## Story 02951
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @LEMMiNO
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_worldbuild_story
- **Video ID:** CbUjuwhQPKs

Just let me know. A Daring parachute escape from applying 727 somewhere between weathe search was made of the plane immediately. We don't know who he was. Very came from a hurry when. I expect to will keep looking until we find him or find out what happened. In the afternoon of November the 24th 1971, a middle-aged man carrying a briefcase walked into Portland International Airport and purchased a one-way ticket to Seattle, Washington. The man identified himself as Dan Cooper and along with 36 other passengers and a crew of six, he soon boarded Northwest Airlines Flight 305. Once aboard Cooper made himself comfortable in the middle of the last row of seats on the right side of the cabin, he ordered a drink and had a smoke because this was the 70s. Once the flight was cleared for the departure, Cooper turned around and handed an envelope to flight attendant Florence Schaffner. Inside the envelope was a note featuring a handwritten message stating he had a bomb. Schaffner reluctantly sat down beside him and glimpsed what appeared to be eight sticks of dynamite inside his briefcase. Cooper's demands were quite simple. He wanted $200,000 in cash and four parachutes. He also demanded a fuel truck to stand ready to refuel the aircraft once they landed in Seattle. Should they fail to comply with his demands, he threatened to quote, do the job. Once the flight was airborne, Schaffner went to inform the cockpit crew while another flight attendant by the name of Tina Mucklow remained by Cooper's side. By using a telephone in the rear of the cabin, Mucklow acted as an intermediary between Cooper and the rest of the flight crew for the remainder of the hijacking. For the next hour and a half, flight through five maintained a holding pat near Seattle while local and federal authorities scrambled to procure the ransom as well as the four parachutes. $10,020 bills were collected from a local bank while the owner of a nearby skydiving school supplied the shoots. At 5.45 pm, more than two hours past its scheduled arrival, flight through five finally touched down in Seattle. By this point, it was well after sunset and the aircraft was brought to remote section of the tarmac. Once the flight came to a stop, both the ransom and the parachutes were handed over to Mucklow who then brought them back aboard. In exchange, Cooper permitted two of the flight attendants as well as all the passengers to disembark, many of whom had not yet realized the flight had been hijacked. With the ransom paid and only four crew members remaining on board, Cooper told Mucklow to inform the captain that he wanted to fly to Mexico City. They were to fly with a landing gear down, the flaps at 15 degrees and below 10,000 feet. The lights in the cabin were to be switched off and the aft stairway which opens from the underbelly of the fuselage was to remain extended. Two of Cooper's demands could not be satisfied. First of all, the flight configuration he requested would not allow for a non-stop flight to Mexico City. As such, Cooper proposed a refueling stop in Phoenix, Yuma or Sacramento before they all agreed on Reno, Nevada. Second of all, it was not possible to depart with a ventral staircase extended. Cooper agreed to retract the stairs on the condition that Mucklow remained by his side and taught him how to extend them was the plane was airborne. Parked for nearly two hours due to complications with refueling, flight 3 of 5 was back in the air by 736 pm. Less than five minutes of the takeoff, Cooper told Mucklow to head for the cockpit and at from this point onwards he was not to be disturbed. The last time she saw Cooper, he was standing in the middle of the aisle as if though he was preparing to jump. Mucklow joined the rest of the crew, locked the cockpit door behind her and some three hours later, flight 3 of 5 safely landed in Reno. Once the flight came to a stop, the crew carefully ventured into the rear of the cabin but there was no sign of Cooper nor the bomb. The aft stairway had been extended mid-flight and was slightly damaged upon landing. It seemed that there was only one explanation for the hijackers absence. At some point between Seattle and Reno, Cooper had strapped on a parachute, walked down the stairs and leaped into the dark of night. As soon as it became clear that Cooper was no longer on board, dozens of FBI agents converged upon the aircraft only to discover a disappointing amount of physical evidence. A black clip on Ty? 8 cigarette butts and 2 of the 4 parachutes were all that Cooper left behind. Evidently he'd brought the ransom and briefcase along with him. In interviews conducted on the night of the hijacking, Cooper was described by the crew and passengers as a white male with brown eyes and dark hair. On the 4th of January, 2012, a user on 4chan posted this image to decide St. Phymus B or Random Board. The anonymous author who went by the 4 digit pseudonym 33-1 challenged users to uncover a message hidden within the image. Unbeknownst to those who stumbled across it, someone had just said in motion one of the most elaborate scavenger hunts the internet has ever seen. Within minutes of the image being posted, someone discovered that by opening the file using a text editor, an appended string of readable text could be found. The string contained a cipher that once desiphered formed a link to yet another image. At first, this appeared to be a dead end, but using an application known as OutGuess, users were able to extract hidden information embedded within the first image. The extracted information led to a subreddit which in turn contained information about the book. The book, along with a code, could then be used to uncover a phone number that when called, played this pre-recorded message. By the following day, the initial image had been reposted all over the internet. A growing community of armchair detectives saw to unravel this elaborate puzzle, but no one was quite sure what to make of it. What was the puzzle for? Who was behind it? What happens when you reach the end? Some naturally dismissed it as an elaborate joke while others perceived its complexity as evidence against it being the work of a mere troll. Before long rumors began to circulate that this could be the work of some secret society or intelligence agency with intent of recruiting individuals proficient in cryptography, stegonography and other related fields. Of course, it was nothing but a rumor. The two missing numbers mentioned in the recording proved to be the dimensions of the original image. After multiplying the width and height with 33 or 1 and using the product as a web address, users were taken to a website. The website consisted of a countdown as well as an image of a cicada. When the countdown reached zero, the page was updated with a list of coordinates. The coordinates pointed to locations around the globe, 14 locations in five different countries. It was now up to participants living near the specified coordinates to rise from their comfortable arm chairs and venture outside. Those who believed cicada to be the work of an organization now felt their beliefs had been justified. In their opinion, only some international collective possessed the means and resources to create a scavenger hunt of this magnitude. This was not the work of your average troll. Now this had to be something else. With each location was a poster with a cicada symbol and a QR code. The codes linked to an image, the image contained a riddle, the riddle led to a book and the book led to a website. But here, the puzzle took an unexpected turn. Only a select group of first arrivals to this website were accepted into the final stage of the puzzle. The site eventually closed down with a message, we want the best, not the followers. The finalists were also warned not to collaborate with others nor to share the details of this private stage of the puzzle. Well, given that we know this, it's safe to say that not everyone heated that warning. But those who did, presumably advanced through the final stages before reaching the very end of the puzzle. After nearly a month of silence, an image appeared on the subreddit announcing the conclusion of the puzzle and just like that, the hunt was over. Cicada had supposedly found that the highly intelligent individuals they were looking for and whatever happened to them is a bit of a mystery, but more on that in a moment. The complete lack of an explanation was perceived by many as confirmation that the puzzle had been nothing but a wild goose chase intent on wasting everyone's time. After all, questions raised by the original image remained unanswered. What was the puzzle for? Who was behind it? What happens when you reach the end? However, as it later turned out, this was only the beginning. Whoever was behind this intricate game had the foresight to include an authentication code known as a PGP signature along with every clue. This allowed users to verify that an image or message was actually from Cicada as supposed to some imposter seeking to derail or hijack the puzzle. Cicada had repeatedly warned of such false paths and insisted that any message lacking a valid PGP signature should promptly be disregarded. That's why this image posted exactly a year and a day after the first provoked such a frenzy. After a year of lackluster imitations, this image finally matched the official PGP signature. Cicada was back and it was time for round two. The second puzzle was not too dissimilar from the first. The image enclosed a message, the message led to a book, the book produced a link, and gradually the puzzle unfolded. At one point a recording title the in-star emergence was uncovered. Just let me know. Near the end of the 16th century, a man by the name of John White embarked on a transatlantic voyage. His destination was the island of Roanoke along the southeast and coast of North America. On this island, White had established an English colony some three years before, and he was now returning to resume his position as governor. After a long and difficult journey, White finally reached the site of the colony, only to find the more than 100 men, women and children he'd left behind and disappeared. A secret message carved into a tree was one of the only clues left behind at the scene. Before White had a chance to conduct a more extensive search, the ship returned to England and in its wake it left a mystery. That happened to the lost colony of Roanoke. Before we can tackle the lost colony, we first need to understand events leading up to its disappearance. The story begins in 1584, when two ships sailed across the Atlantic to scout for a suitable location for the planting of the first permanent English colony in America. In mid-July, they made landfall on a string of barrier islands known as the Outer Banks. The English quickly developed friendly relations with the local Native Americans and were soon invited to their village on Roanoke Island. After about a month, the scouting expedition returned to their ships and sailed back to England. Upon the expedition's return in late September, the man in charge of the Enterprise Walter Raleigh immediately began preparations to plant a permanent colony somewhere along the Outer Banks. The colony was soon thereafter knighted by Queen Elizabeth I and the new land was to be named Virginia in honor of the Virgin Queen. In April of 1585, a fleet of seven ships departed England with a complement of roughly 600 men and loaded with enough provisions to sustain the colony for about a year. The fleet reached the Outer Banks at the end of June but was soon faced with a crisis. Navigating the waters of the Outer Banks can be notoriously treacherous as indicated by these sinking ships and when the flagship passed through one of the shallow inlets, it struck a shell and was nearly destroyed. As the flagship carried the bulk of the provisions and most of it was now spoiled by sea water, the scope of the colony had to be dramatically reduced. About 100 men, far fewer than initially intended, were stationed on Roanoke Island and with the native's approval, they began construction of what was to become the Roanoke colony. The island was primarily chosen for its strategic value. It provided quick access to the ocean while still making the colony invisible to Spanish patrols. You see, Spain had already colonized and laid claim to much of what the English now called Virginia, so they had to be careful not to attract any unwanted attention. A second wave of supplies and reinforcements was expected to arrive before the winter but unbeknownst to the colonists, the resupply mission had been countermanded by the queen to deal with more pressing concerns back in England. As such, once the colonists ran out of food, they had to rely on the generosity of the natives. However, the natives only had so much to spare and struggled to meet increasing demands by the colonists. This overdependence quickly began to strain their relationship. And while people had begun to notice other stir-bing trend, every time the English visited a native American village, many of its inhabitants would inexplicably collapse and die. The natives believed the English could kill from a distance by shooting invisible bullets and they were not too far off. While unknown at the time, the English were carrying deadly pathogens to which the natives lacked immunity and thus an epidemic was unwittingly unleashed upon the indigenous population. The two leaders back on Roanoke, colonial governor Ralph Lane and Indian chiefs and Wenzina eventually grew so suspicious of one another that cohabitation was no longer possible. Wenzina decided to remove his people from the island and retreated to a larger village on the mainland. Meanwhile, Lane came to believe that Wenzina had formed an alliance with other tribes and that they were plotting to launch an attack against the colony. Whether he was paranoid or not, Lane decided to take preemptive action. On the 1st of June 1586, Lane and his men made their way to the mainland village and mass occurred its people. One of the Englishmen chased after and decapitated Wenzina and his head was later impaled outside the fort of the colony. A week later, a large English fleet commanded by the renowned sea captain Sir Francis Drake dropped by the outer banks on its way back to England. Given the lack of food and violent clashes with the natives, it was decided to abandon the colony. The colonists were hastily evacuated off the island and then after nine long months, they all returned home. All except three who were not located in time for the evacuation and so the fleet left the

This form of positional tracking depends on a signal being emitted by one of two transponders aboard a plane, and so its disappearance would suggest both transponders cease to function, or the system was manually deleted. All subsequent attempts to contact and ascertain the wearabats of Flight 370 are unsuccessful. The aircraft has seemingly vanished without the trace. The search was initially concentrated around the location of the flight's disappearance between the South China Sea and the Gulf of Thailand. The search area was soon expanded however after the Malaysian military disclosed additional information. Unlike the radar system employed by air traffic control, long range military radar does not rely on transponders but use reflectance to track the position of aerial targets. Meanwhile, investigators began to analyze the aircraft's satellite communication records. Like all modern airliners, Flight 370 was equipped with a satellite communications terminal or SATCOM to send and receive transmissions to and from the ground. Prior to the departure, the SATCOM terminal had logged on to the satellite network and established a connection with a ground station in Perth, Australia. That station then maintained a detailed record of all the incoming and outgoing traffic between it and Flight 370. This is what it contained. Prior to the flight's disappearance of the South China Sea, everything appeared to be working as intended. Then at some point, during this portion of the flight, the SATCOM link was severed. For whatever reason, the terminal ceased to respond. But three minutes after the flight vanished over the Andaman Sea, the terminal requested to log back onto the network. The SATCOM link was successfully reestablished and was not disrupted again until nearly six hours later when the flight is presumed to have crashed due to fuel exhaustion. During these final hours, two attempts were made to contact the plane via a satellite telephone. Both calls were acknowledged by the SATCOM terminal and would have been routed to the cockpit. Yet, they went unanswered. The terminal had also responded to five automatic status requests. In short, if the ground station had not heard from the aircraft in over an hour, it would transmit the signal to confirm the terminal was still online. Does that mean no? In the spring of 1806, two German astronomers were independently observing Venus when they noticed something odd. The hemisphere facing away from the sun did not perfectly blend with the blackness of space. Instead, it had a faint glow with a gray, red or ashen gray appearance. This ashen light resembled a phenomenon known as Earth shine. Its when sunlight reflected by the Earth faintly illuminates the night side of the moon. It seemed reasonable therefore to ascribe the ashen light to a similar cause, namely that sunlight reflected by a Venetian moon was faintly illuminating the night side of Venus. Only one slight problem, Venus doesn't have a moon. This meant the ashen light resembled Earth shine in appearance only because there was nothing in proximity of Venus to reflect any sunlight. In the words of famed astronomer William Herschel, those regions that are turned from the sun cannot possibly shine by a borrowed light. A few decades later, another German astronomer, François Corteson, proposed a rather creative solution to this mystery. The ashen light was for a result of sweeping wildfires on the surface of Venus deliberately ignited by its inhabitants. As absurd as that sounds, there were tantalizing similarities between Venus and Earth. The two planets were of similar size, and much like the Earth, Venus had an atmosphere, a densely cloud-laden atmosphere which gave astronomers and science-fiction authors alike, free reign to speculate about what could lie beneath. A bear and desert, a planet-wide ocean, a tropical rainforest, anything was possible. It was in this context that Corteson made his proposal in 1836. He believed that Venus was inhabited by a humanoid species who would periodically light fires in the surface, the purpose of which was either to clear land, to commemorate a religious occasion, or to celebrate the coronation of a new Venetian Emperor. Corteson did perhaps get a bit carried away, but he was far from the first to suggest that Venus was inhabited, titans of astronomy like Christian Huygensen, William Herschel, and visioned all the planets, including Venus, to be teaming with life. It may take centuries, it might take eons, but extinction is unavoidable. The future is fraught with uncertainty, and the apocalypse never seems too far away. Nearly 800,000 years ago, an approximately 1 km wide asteroid collided with Earth. The crater has yet to be located, but fragments from the impact have been found scattered across the east and hemisphere, an expansive, strewn field that covers up to 30% of the planet's surface. Our distant ancestors were not only alive to witness this event, but managed to adapt and survive. Impact of this magnitude occurred roughly 1.5 million to 2 million years. More devastating impacts, like the one that nearly wiped out the dinosaurs, are far less frequent. Possibly striking 1,7,100 million years. Whether a collision of this magnitude is survivable is a matter of debate. The kinetic energy released upon impact would be equivalent to the simultaneous detonation of billions of atomic bombs. Those who survived initial blast would be subjected to unrelenting earthquakes, firestorms and tsunamis. A thick layer of smoke and dust would quickly envelop the globe and blot out the sun, an impact winter that could last for months or even years. Darkness, freezing cold and global famine would throw the world into chaos. In spite of this, long-term survival is not inconceivable. Small pockets of humanity may take refuge underground and would at least be given a chance to rebuild civilization. But make no mistake, it would be a close call. The survivability of an impact also depends on how much time we have to prepare. More than 90% of nearby asteroids and comets larger than 1 kilometer have already been detected. If one of them were on a collision course with Earth, we'd know for decades or even centuries in advance. But this was not always the case. In 1983, a 9 kilometer wide comet was only spotted two weeks prior to its closest approach. That's two weeks of preparation had its been aiming for Earth. While things have significantly improved since 1983, space is impossibly vast. In 2019, an asteroid capable of leveling a city was detected a day before it flew five times closer to the Earth than the Moon. While the odds of an extinction-level collision are remote, it's not impossible. There is a class of objects known as Damacloids. Damacloids are typified by their highly eccentric orbits and low reflectivity, meaning they periodically pay us a visit and are extremely difficult to detect. We're currently tracking about 200, but there could be hundreds more, tumbling through the solar system. Damacloids are, on average, 16 kilometers wide. If one of them were on a collision course with Earth, there might not be any warning. When massive stars die, they explode. If a star went supernova within 30 light years of Earth, it would be cost for concern. If a star went supernova within 30 light years of Earth, it would be cost for concern. Fortunately, more than 90% of the stars within this bubble have already been discovered. None of which are at risk of exploding anytime soon. But some supernovae can still pose a threat from thousands of light years away. It's a gamma ray burst, produced by only the most violent explosions in the Universe. Instead of a spherical blast, a gamma ray burst will channel the explosion into two highly concentrated beams. More energies released in a few seconds than the Sun will radiate in its entire lifetime. The biological effects of a nearby supernova or gamma ray burst are poorly understood. We do know that the atmosphere would absorb a much of the high energy radiation. But this may also strip the Earth of its protective layer of ozone. If so, harmful levels of ultraviolet radiation from the Sun would penetrate down to the surface. Certain chemical reactions may also darken the sky and induce global cooling, a cosmic winter of sorts. This could last for years and would be lethal to many plants and animals. In fact, a gamma ray burst might be responsible for a mass extinction that killed roughly 70% of all marine species nearly half a billion years ago. Now, humanity could easily protect itself against ultraviolet sun rays, but living through a mass extinction would not be a pleasant experience. While some authors have painted a far bleaker image with one hemisphere effectively sterilized by intense radiation, these findings have been contested in recent years. The latest research identify ozone depletion as the primary threat. Nevertheless, a gamma ray burst sniping the Earth from across the galaxy is extremely improbable. All of them have so far been extra galactic in origin, much too distant to pose a threat. While there are a few potential candidates within the Milky Way, none of them appear to be directed at Earth. In human terms, that's about 169, just like the woman's height relative to the probe's diameter. I remember in your Q&A back in 2015 that you didn't want to do this YouTube thing forever, does that idea still stand almost four years later? If not, then what would you want to pursue? As I've said in the past, I'll keep making videos as long as I enjoy making them but if I were to quit right now, I'd probably go back to school and maybe get an arts degree. Either that or apply to become a Mars colonist, I imagine the employment rate is pretty much the same. Without the years of making videos, what have you learned during this experience that you'd love to have known already from the start and can you give any suggestions as to how to get into video editing for someone who wants to get into it but doesn't really know where to start? Well, if I've learned anything making videos for the past seven years, it's that it's important to choose a good channel name, which is why I named my channel Top 10 Memes. As for your second question, when I switched from Sony Vegas to Premiere Pro some years back, I began by watching very specialized tutorials. I'll make text, tutorial, how make text, move, tutorial, how show, image, tutorial, how make image, tutorial, I think you get the idea. Instead of watching a 20 part series or something that cover virtually every aspect of everything there is to know all at once, I just figure out how to do exactly what it is you want to do and I promise you, you will learn everything you need to know along the way. And that's what worked for me at least. Yes, even you with a lot of hard work can learn masterclass editing like this. One of your favorite YouTube channels and do you have any favorite documentary series? I love what Tom Scott is doing, Pauli Matter is great, went over productions, Captain the Solution, all Shift X, Lindsay Ellis, all of them make fantastic videos. As for documentaries, I highly recommend the Vietnam War by Ken Burns and Linnavich and not the most uplifting documentary but it's very well made. What do you do when you're not making videos? Absolutely nothing at all. It is the best. In fact I think I'm gonna go and do that right now, well as soon as I'm done with these questions of course. Do you do this full time if not is it far from becoming a full time job would you ever consider hiring people to help you upload more frequently? This isn't even my full time job and thanks to my lovely patrons I'm doing just fine. I've thought about expanding and hiring employees but there is something to be said for being your own boss. I wish I could upload more frequently but it is what it is. Once one topic you don't feel qualified to make a video about, well as you saw before my math skills are very impressive so I won't touch anything with a lot of complicated math. Mathematics is not my strong suit and I find no joy in working with numbers. I wish I was better at it but I'm not gonna put in the time. I've tried to talk about quantum mechanics a couple of times in the past and I hear by solemnly swear to never ever attempt that again. Rate my cat. Yeah, pretty decent cat. See this is more my league, this is what I'm qualified to talk about. What inspired you to

## Story 02968
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @LEMMiNO
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_worldbuild_story
- **Video ID:** zy4ay9xfKpQ

Just let me know. Oceans cover a significant portion of the earth and as the spin off a fish, it's no surprise that mankind really loves the ocean. It allows us to cool off in the summer, produce life-giving oxygen, and much like whales can often be found below sea level. Because of this, we have learned to treat the ocean with a care and respect, such a delicate life-essential system deserves. It's what I would have said had this been a parallel universe in which things made sense. Unfortunately, we live in the other universe. Because the ocean is going through a bit of a rough patch at the moment, fellow creators Mr. Beast and Mark Rober have launched a campaign known as Team Seas. It's a kind of sequel to the Team Trees initiative which successfully raised more than 20 million dollars to plant over 20 million trees back in 2019. But this time around, the goal is to raise 30 million dollars to remove 30 million pounds of trash and plastic from the ocean, beaches and rivers. Which is why almost everyone you follow seems to develop a very sudden passion for oceanography. In the grand scheme of things, removing 30 million pounds of trash and plastic is not going to change the world. Billions of pounds of trash is dumped into the ocean every single year. It will, however, bring awareness to an issue which affects every single one of us. And perhaps with some luck that awareness will inspire those with a power to effect lasting change. If not today, then perhaps tomorrow. Who knows? I think it's a worthy cause at least, so I will be donating $1,000. If you'd like to donate yourself, you should head over to Team Seas.org. You'll find the link with additional information in the description below.

---

foreign [Applause] days mom treated me to a Disney cruise for the first two days we started going to Family karaoke and the crowd bear was pretty General audience some adults some kids on the third night however I was looking through the song book and began to notice some of these songs seemed a little inappropriate for Family karaoke but hey it's Disney they must know what they're doing I picked a country song that I was fully allowed to sing as a kid called Goodbye Earl I got up and started singing no problem but then the song got to the chorus and if you're unfamiliar it goes a little something like this cause Earl had to die so on and so forth as soon as I belted those magical words it's like someone hit the brakes on the show the music cut out the lights came up the host rushed up to the stage and very anxiously said into her microphone we're very sorry but a mistake was made that song was not supposed to be in the book please sit down I turned beet red immediately left and never darkened the doorway of another family karaoke ever again

## Story 02970
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @LetMeExplainStudios
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_animated_story
- **Video ID:** g1MoG7nkzA4

foreign [Applause] time during the summer in between animation school I was helping out at one of my parents self storage facilities we had an auction one day and we opened up one of the units to find it completely empty except for a lone baseball bat sitting in the middle of the floor no one wanted to give it a good home so I scooped it up and took it back with me to college when I showed it to one of my roommates she said this bat needs a name and I said well how about you name it and this truly felt like a main character moment in a video game where your mighty sword is about to receive a blessing because my roommate got a serious look on her face held the battle Loft and said Pedro and to this day I still have Pedro he sits right next to my bed in case of emergencies I haven't needed to use his full power yet but there have been a handful of occasions I tiptoed around my own house with Pedro in hand wondering what made that spooky noise

## Story 02971
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @LetMeExplainStudios
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_animated_story
- **Video ID:** grXy-ZKB7bo

foreign [Applause] random day I was at the grocery store by myself naive unsuspecting oblivious to the awkwardness that's about to happen when suddenly a young guy approached me and said excuse me but I couldn't help but notice that you aren't wearing a wedding ring I looked down at my hands and sure enough this observant Young fella has a fair point I was Sans the wedding band I said yeah I'm not married that's when he looked me dead in the eye and said well I was wondering if you'd be interested in having a boyfriend that's quite a sales pitch you got there my man excuse me little lady are you plague with crippling loneliness do you find yourself staying in every Friday night with no one to talk to well have I got the solution for you I call it having me as your boyfriend what do you say uh no thank you and he just said okay and walked away hey I give him full credit for respecting my wishes but by gum that boy woke up in the morning and said I'm gonna shoot my shot and he did best of luck to you dude

foreign [Applause] it has come to my attention that this old bit of Animation I did back in 2014 has become a bit of a viral meme can't imagine why is before I became a full-time YouTuber I used to do freelance work for clients and one of those clients was a company called number rock they specialize in making educational math songs for kids I animated a few videos for them and this is one of them I did not write the song however that was all on them that is their sin to answer for but I remember listening to the song for the first time and hearing the lyrics this angle's kind of fat it's got a Big Caboose and I thought to myself if I don't animate an enormous jiggling butt for this I will regret it for the rest of my life you can't just give me the words Big Caboose and expect me to do nothing I wiggled my booty in the mirror a lot to get the motion just right I showed the final product number rock and they completely approved it it is their fault not mine and if you still don't believe me here's the flash file

## Story 02973
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @LetMeExplainStudios
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_animated_story
- **Video ID:** KVR55g02Hno

I have a cursed karaoke song when I was in animation School my roommates and I would sometimes go out to the local karaoke bar well one night we arrived in a group of young 20 something actors from the local theater scene were already there jamming out to show tunes given everyone was in a musical theater mood already I signed up to sing one of my favorite Disney songs feed the birds from Mary Poppins but when I got up and started singing I began hearing really loud obnoxious bird sounds coming from the audience I looked out and it was the theater kids deliberately taunting me and trying to disrupt my song I tried to ignore them in finished strong but when I sat back down I couldn't help but feel embarrassed for the rest of the night so a year later at a different karaoke place I was determined to take that song back but when the music started it wasn't the instrumental track but the demo track with a Cheesy Voice singing over me I waved at the DJ to stop and correct the issue but he didn't seem to care and every single time I have ever tried to sing that song for karaoke since something has gone wrong until today once and for all I shall break this curse

foreign the 90s kids out there will probably remember Tamagotchis the insanely popular digital pets that would die if you didn't clean up their poop and no pause button on these things so you had to take them everywhere with you including school where they would chirp their little demands at you all day so you can imagine a kid might periodically want to break from this needy piece of plastic well one day in the fifth grade a girl came up to me at recess while I was sitting at the picnic tables by myself she said so Rebecca you're a weirdo loner kid doing nothing at recess mind watching my Tamagotchi for me quarter what you heard me 25 cents fine and that was the humble beginnings of Rebecca's Tamagotchi Babysitting Service just 25 cents I keep your pixel children alive or your money back I had a sign and everything I was making two or three dollars a day and that's nothing to sneeze up for an elementary school kid when sodas were 50 cents a pop at the vending machines I may not have been the most popular kid in class but that year I was certainly the most industrious

I swapped my smartphone for a flip phone for 30 days. If you're anything like me, you're tired of letting your phone run your life. First thing in the morning, phone on the toilet, phone, waiting for coffee, phone. So, I decided to go full digital detox and see if a flip phone could make me happier. The good, I wasn't doom scrolling every spare second. I found myself more present and more patient. The bad, texting took a really long time. I basically became a caveman. K was my new love language. and the GPS completely useless. I had to print directions like it was 2005. But the biggest downside that I didn't expect in the beginning was that I stopped capturing memories. My smartphone had become my camera, my journal, my map. The trade-off between convenience and happiness wasn't as simple as I thought. A flip phone might not be realistic for most of us, but I found it to be a great experiment to reset my scrolling habits. But maybe it'll work for you.

That's it. Once the time's up, you can stop. No pressure, no commitment, just 5 minutes. Sounds too simple, right? But here's the thing. The hardest part of any task isn't actually doing it. It's overcoming the mental friction that stops you from starting. Let me ask you a question. Which of these sounds more doable? Build a successful YouTube channel that pulls in hundreds of thousands of views per month or spend 5 minutes coming up with new video ideas for your channel. He says, "If you don't want to do something, make a deal with yourself to do it for at least 5 minutes. After 5 minutes, you'll end up doing the whole thing. So, next time you're stuck, try setting a timer for 5 minutes.
